8.1.title: Arjuna's six interrogatives open the chapter
8.1.body: The verse stacks six किम् pronouns — किं तद्ब्रह्म किमध्यात्मं किं कर्म ... अधिभूतं किं ... अधिदैवं किम् — each a प्रथमा नपुंसक interrogative demanding a definitional reply. प्रोक्तम् and उच्यते are passive forms (कर्मणि प्रयोग), signalling that Arjuna is asking which technical term has been declared by tradition for each cosmic level.

8.1.title: पुरुषोत्तम as the chapter's first epithet
8.1.body: Arjuna addresses Krishna as पुरुषोत्तम (सम्बोधन एकवचन) — पुरुष + उत्तम, "highest among puruṣas" — choosing precisely the term that the Akṣara-Brahma chapter will end by unpacking. The vocative is not action-bearing; it frames the questions as directed to the one whose own ontological status is at issue.

8.2.title: अधियज्ञः कथं कः — the seventh question
8.2.body: The compound interrogative कथम् + कः asks both "how" and "who" the अधियज्ञ is, located by अत्र देहे ऽस्मिन् (सप्तमी एकवचन — locative of bodily indwelling). The shift from "what" (किम्) to "who" (कः) anticipates 8.4's answer अधियज्ञो ऽहम् — a personal, not abstract, principle.

8.2.title: प्रयाणकाले — the death-moment as epistemic test
8.2.body: प्रयाणकाले is सप्तमी एकवचन ("at the time of departure"), and ज्ञेयः is a future passive participle ("how are you to be known"). नियतात्मभिः is तृतीया बहुवचन — "by those whose self is restrained" — agentive instrumental that frames the entire remaining chapter as an answer for disciplined practitioners.

8.3.title: Three definitions stacked by उच्यते / संज्ञितः
8.3.body: Krishna answers in compressed apposition: अक्षरं ब्रह्म परमम् (subject), स्वभावः अध्यात्मम् उच्यते, and विसर्गः कर्मसंज्ञितः. The passive verbs उच्यते (लट् कर्मणि) and the बहुव्रीहि compound कर्मसंज्ञितः ("that whose designation is karma") frame each definition as a received technical equation, not a description.

8.3.title: भूतभावोद्भवकरो विसर्गः — karma as world-emitting act
8.3.body: विसर्गः ("releasing, emission") is qualified by the तत्पुरुष compound भूत-भाव-उद्भव-करः — "the maker of the arising of the existence of beings." This unpacks कर्म as not mere ritual or moral act but the very ontological emission that brings beings into manifestation, anchoring later sāṃkhya discussions.

8.4.title: अधिभूतं क्षरो भावः — the perishing substrate
8.4.body: अधिभूतम् is identified with क्षरः भावः — "perishable existence," using क्षर as विशेषण to भाव. The masculine पुरुषः is then declared अधिदैवतम् (नपुंसक predicate — gender mismatch resolved by predicate noun convention), distinguishing the cosmic Person from the perishing field.

8.4.title: अधियज्ञो ऽहम् एव — Krishna identifies himself
8.4.body: The seventh question gets a first-person answer: अधियज्ञः अहम् एव अत्र देहे, with अहम् in प्रथमा and the एव particle excluding any alternative. देहभृताम् वर (सम्बोधन — "O best of body-bearers") is precisely chosen: Krishna locates the यज्ञ-principle in the body, addressing Arjuna as one who himself bears a body.

8.5.title: स्मरन् मुक्त्वा प्रयाति — three converbal stages
8.5.body: The verse threads three non-finite forms before the finite verb: स्मरन् (शतृ — "remembering"), मुक्त्वा (क्त्वा absolutive — "having released"), and finally प्रयाति (लट् प्रथम एकवचन — finite anchor). The participle plus absolutive plus finite sequence encodes the entire death-moment as one continuous yogic act.

8.5.title: मद्भावं याति — the result clause's bold equation
8.5.body: मद्भावम् (द्वितीया एकवचन — accusative of goal with याति) is glossed by नास्ति अत्र संशयः ("there is no doubt here") — Krishna's signature certainty-clause. The accusative of motion with याति preserves Sanskrit's preference for the goal as the direct object of "going," without preposition.

8.6.title: यं यं ... तं तम् — the correlative of last thought
8.6.body: The doubled relative-correlative यं यं ... तं तम् (द्वितीया एकवचन, distributive reduplication) means "whatever ... that very same." स्मरन् भावम् त्यजति is the relative clause; तम् तम् एव एति is the main clause. The एव seals the identity: there is no slippage between final thought and final destination.

8.6.title: सदा तद्भावभावितः — the conditioning compound
8.6.body: तद्भावभावितः is a tatpuruṣa-bahuvrīhi: "made-to-have-the-state-of-that," with भावित (क्त) from णिच् causative of √भू — "caused to become." The सदा ("always") rules out a deathbed shortcut: the final thought reflects a lifetime of conditioning, not last-minute substitution.

8.7.title: तस्मात् — Krishna's practical conclusion
8.7.body: तस्मात् (पञ्चमी ablative-of-reason) draws the consequence from 8.5–6: since the final thought determines destination, मामनुस्मर युध्य च — two लोट् imperatives (अनुस्मर is अनु + √स्मृ, युध्य is √युध् आत्मनेपद imperative). The conjunction च yokes remembering and fighting as one practice, not alternatives.

8.7.title: मय्यर्पितमनोबुद्धिः — the bahuvrīhi yogi
8.7.body: मय्यर्पित-मनो-बुद्धिः is a बहुव्रीहि: "one whose mind-and-intellect have been deposited in me," with अर्पित (क्त past passive participle) and मयि (सप्तमी locative of deposit). The compound describes the subject of एष्यसि (लृट् मध्यम एकवचन future — "you will come"); असंशयम् (अव्यय) reinforces certainty.

8.8.title: अभ्यासयोगयुक्तेन चेतसा — the instrumental of practice
8.8.body: चेतसा (तृतीया एकवचन — instrumental) is the means of going; it is qualified by the long तत्पुरुष अभ्यास-योग-युक्त ("yoked-to-the-yoga-of-practice") and by नान्यगामिना (बहुव्रीहि — "not going elsewhere"). Two qualifiers, one instrumental — the verse insists the mind must be both trained and undistracted.

8.8.title: अनुचिन्तयन् याति — the participle of approach
8.8.body: अनुचिन्तयन् (शतृ — "continuously thinking on") modifies the implicit subject of याति (लट् — "goes"). परमं पुरुषं दिव्यम् is the द्वितीया goal (three matching masculine accusatives, सामानाधिकरण्य). The śatṛ-participle plus finite construction makes meditation simultaneous with motion, not prior to it.

8.9.title: Six accusative epithets governed by अनुस्मरेत्
8.9.body: The verse piles up द्वितीया एकवचन epithets — कविम्, पुराणम्, अनुशासितारम्, अणोरणीयांसम्, धातारम्, अचिन्त्यरूपम्, आदित्यवर्णम् — all governed by the optional विधिलिङ् verb अनुस्मरेत् ("one should constantly recall"). The सामानाधिकरण्य is total: six masculine accusatives qualify one implicit Person.

8.9.title: अणोरणीयांसम् — the comparative of subtlety
8.9.body: अणोः (पञ्चमी ablative of comparison) + अणीयांसम् (comparative -ईयस् of अणु — "smaller than the small"). The Sanskrit comparative governs ablative, exactly as Latin does. तमसः परस्तात् ("beyond darkness") closes the verse with another ablative — the Person is at once smallest and beyond the largest obscurity.

8.10.title: प्राणम् आवेश्य — the absolutive of placement
8.10.body: आवेश्य is the ल्यप् absolutive (आ + √विश् causative) — "having caused to enter." प्राणम् (द्वितीया) is its object; भ्रुवोर्मध्ये (सप्तमी locative of the dual eyebrows) is the destination. The technical yogic instruction is grammatically a converbal clause subordinated to the main verb उपैति.

8.10.title: तृतीयाs of yogic equipment
8.10.body: Three instrumentals stack up: मनसा अचलेन ("with an unshaken mind"), भक्त्या युक्तः ("yoked by devotion"), and योगबलेन ("by the power of yoga"). The verse demonstrates Sanskrit's stacking of तृतीया to enumerate co-operating means — none subordinated, all converging on स तं परं पुरुषमुपैति दिव्यम् (the only finite verb).

8.11.title: Three relative clauses define the यद् पद
8.11.body: The verse builds three parallel यद् clauses — यद् वेदविदः वदन्ति, यद् यतयः विशन्ति, यद् इच्छन्तः ब्रह्मचर्यं चरन्ति — all relative to the same neuter accusative पदम्, recovered by the correlative तत् in pāda d. The triple relativization defines the goal by three groups: scholars who speak it, ascetics who enter it, celibates who seek it.

8.11.title: सङ्ग्रहेण प्रवक्ष्ये — the promise of compression
8.11.body: प्रवक्ष्ये is लृट् उत्तम एकवचन आत्मनेपद ("I will declare for myself / I shall announce"), of √वच् with future ष्य-infix. सङ्ग्रहेण (तृतीया — "by way of summary") frames the next two verses as a deliberately condensed exposition of the OṂ technique.

8.12.title: Three absolutives sequence the yogic gates
8.12.body: संयम्य ("having restrained," सर्वद्वाराणि acc. pl.), निरुध्य ("having confined," मनः acc. + हृदि loc.), and आधाय ("having placed," प्राणम् acc. + मूर्ध्नि loc.) — three ल्यप् absolutives in sequence, each with object plus locative, build the entire preparatory ritual as subordinate converbs before the main verb (which arrives only in 8.13).

8.12.title: योगधारणाम् आस्थितः — the past-passive anchor
8.12.body: आस्थितः (क्त past passive participle of आ + √स्था) describes the practitioner as "having seated himself in." योगधारणाम् (द्वितीया एकवचन — the technical "yogic concentration") is its object. The क्त form here is not finite but agrees with the implicit subject continued into the next verse, an enjambment rare in śloka.

8.13.title: ओम् इति एकाक्षरं ब्रह्म — the syllable as Brahman
8.13.body: एकाक्षरम् ("single-syllabled," विशेषण to ब्रह्म) names OṂ as a textual instance of Brahman. व्याहरन् (शतृ — "uttering") and अनुस्मरन् (शतृ — "continuously remembering") are simultaneous participles modifying the relative-clause subject यः, who then प्रयाति त्यजन् देहम्.

8.13.title: परमां गतिम् — the accusative of attained goal
8.13.body: परमाम् गतिम् is द्वितीया एकवचन स्त्रीलिंग with याति. The यः ... स ... structure (relative-correlative) makes the entire conditional explicit: one who departs while uttering OṂ and remembering Krishna — that one goes to the highest course. The two śatṛ-participles function as adverbial conditions, not as a verb sequence.

8.14.title: अनन्यचेताः — the bahuvrīhi of single attention
8.14.body: अनन्यचेताः is a बहुव्रीहि ("one whose mind is not other") in प्रथमा एकवचन agreeing with यः. सततं ... नित्यशः doubles the temporal — "constantly ... always" — a quasi-tautology that the metre absorbs but the rhetoric uses to drum exclusivity.

8.14.title: तस्य सुलभः — the genitive of ease
8.14.body: तस्य (षष्ठी एकवचन) is the genitive of the person to whom Krishna is easily attained; सुलभः is a predicate adjective with implicit अस्मि. नित्ययुक्तस्य योगिनः is in apposition with तस्य (षष्ठी), tripling the genitive: "for that one, for the ever-yoked, for the yogin — I am easily attained."

8.15.title: मामुपेत्य — the absolutive of arrival
8.15.body: मामुपेत्य (माम् + उप + √इ ल्यप् absolutive — "having approached me") is the gateway clause. The main verb न आप्नुवन्ति (लट् प्रथम बहुवचन of √आप्) takes पुनर्जन्म as its accusative object, with दुःखालयम् अशाश्वतम् as two qualifying adjectives in द्वितीया एकवचन नपुंसक.

8.15.title: संसिद्धिं परमां गताः — past participle as predicate
8.15.body: गताः is the masculine plural क्त past participle of √गम्, predicated of महात्मानः; संसिद्धिं परमाम् is द्वितीया ("having gone to ultimate perfection"). The क्त with accusative — Sanskrit's way of saying "those who have attained" — distinguishes a finished state from an ongoing one.

8.16.title: आब्रह्मभुवनात् — the limit-ablative
8.16.body: आब्रह्मभुवनात् is पञ्चमी एकवचन preceded by आ- ("up to, until"), the standard Sanskrit construction for an upper limit. लोकाः are प्रथमा बहुवचन of the finite verb (implicit सन्ति) qualified by पुनरावर्तिनः ("having repeated return"), the very property that the higher path nullifies.

8.16.title: मामुपेत्य तु — the contrastive turn
8.16.body: The तु particle marks the contrast with the preceding clause. मामुपेत्य is again the absolutive of approach; पुनर्जन्म न विद्यते uses विद्यते (लट् कर्मणि — "is found") with negation. The construction makes the absence of rebirth a stative fact, not an action denied.

8.17.title: सहस्रयुगपर्यन्तम् — the cosmological accusative
8.17.body: सहस्रयुगपर्यन्तम् is द्वितीया एकवचन functioning as accusative of duration ("lasting until a thousand yugas"). It qualifies अहर् ("day") which is the object of विदुः (लिट् perfect of √विद् — "they know"). The यद् ... तद् structure relativizes Brahmā's day; तेऽहोरात्रविदः names the knowers.

8.17.title: अहोरात्रविदः — the द्वन्द्व+तत्पुरुष
8.17.body: अहोरात्र is a द्वन्द्व compound ("day-and-night") which then becomes the first member of a तत्पुरुष with -विद् ("knower of day-and-night"). The compound functions as प्रथमा बहुवचन agreeing with जनाः, identifying the cosmologists who have computed Brahmā's diurnal scale.

8.18.title: अव्यक्तात् ... प्रभवन्ति — ablative of origin
8.18.body: अव्यक्तात् (पञ्चमी एकवचन — "from the unmanifest") is the ablative of source for प्रभवन्ति (लट् प्रथम बहुवचन of प्र + √भू — "come forth"). Subject व्यक्तयः सर्वाः ("all manifestations") is स्त्रीलिंग प्रथमा बहुवचन. अहरागमे (सप्तमी — "at day's arrival") gives the temporal anchor.

8.18.title: तत्रैव अव्यक्तसंज्ञके — the recursive locative
8.18.body: तत्र एव is the emphatic locative adverb; अव्यक्तसंज्ञके (सप्तमी एकवचन of a बहुव्रीहि — "in that whose designation is unmanifest") is in apposition. The verse closes the cycle: what emerges from the avyakta returns to that very avyakta, signalled by प्रलीयन्ते (लट् कर्मणि — "are dissolved").

8.19.title: भूत्वा भूत्वा प्रलीयते — reduplicated absolutive
8.19.body: भूत्वा भूत्वा is a doubled क्त्वा absolutive of √भू — "having come into being again and again." The reduplication encodes habitual repetition. The finite verb प्रलीयते (लट् कर्मणि एकवचन of प्र + √ली — "is dissolved") then closes the cycle for भूतग्रामः ("the collective host of beings").

8.19.title: अवशः — the bahuvrīhi of compulsion
8.19.body: अवशः is a प्रथमा एकवचन बहुव्रीहि ("one without control / unwilling") agreeing with the implicit भूतग्रामः. It modifies the dissolution: the host does not choose, it is dissolved. The contrast with the yogin's chosen departure in 8.5 is exactly this अवशता.

8.20.title: परस्तस्मात् — the ablative of transcendence
8.20.body: परः तस्मात् (तस्मात् पञ्चमी एकवचन) means "higher than that," where तस्मात् refers back to 8.18's avyakta. The verse then names a *second* avyakta: अव्यक्तात् अव्यक्तः सनातनः — "unmanifest beyond the unmanifest, eternal." Sanskrit can stack abl. + nom. of the same lexeme to mark two levels of one term.

8.20.title: नश्यत्सु न विनश्यति — the locative absolute
8.20.body: नश्यत्सु (सप्तमी बहुवचन शतृ-participle of √नश्) with सर्वेषु भूतेषु forms a सति-सप्तमी (locative absolute — "when all beings are perishing"). The finite न विनश्यति ("does not perish") names the one survivor. The locative absolute is Sanskrit's standard "when X happens" construction without a finite verb.

8.21.title: अव्यक्तः अक्षरः इत्युक्तः — the appositional iti
8.21.body: इति marks a quotation/designation; उक्तः (क्त past passive participle of √वच्) makes the whole compound predicate: "called avyakta-akṣara." तम् आहुः (लट्/लिट् of √अह् — "they call him") — the verbal repetition makes the naming explicit, with परमाम् गतिम् as the द्वितीया secondary predicate.

8.21.title: तद्धाम परमं मम — the genitive of possession
8.21.body: मम (षष्ठी एकवचन) is the genitive of Krishna; तद्धाम परमम् (नपुंसक प्रथमा एकवचन) is the predicate noun. The relative clause यं प्राप्य न निवर्तन्ते (with प्राप्य ल्यप् absolutive) precedes — "having attained which they do not return." Non-return is the defining property of परम धाम.

8.22.title: भक्त्या ... अनन्यया — instrumental of exclusive devotion
8.22.body: भक्त्या (तृतीया एकवचन) and अनन्यया (matching तृतीया विशेषण — "non-other, exclusive") together give the means of attaining. लभ्यः is a future passive participle ("to be attained") of √लभ्. The तु particle contrasts this verse's path with the cosmological computation of 8.17–20.

8.22.title: Two relative clauses qualify पुरुषः
8.22.body: यस्य अन्तःस्थानि भूतानि (षष्ठी relative — "in whom beings reside") and येन सर्वम् इदं ततम् (तृतीया relative — "by whom all this is pervaded," with ततम् क्त of √तन्). Two relativizations with two different cases: genitive of indwelling, instrumental of pervasion.

8.23.title: यत्र काले — the locative of conditioning time
8.23.body: यत्र काले is सप्तमी एकवचन relative ("at which time"); the correlative तं कालम् (द्वितीया एकवचन — accusative of what is to be spoken) is governed by वक्ष्यामि (लृट् उत्तम एकवचन of √वच् — "I shall declare"). The relative-correlative bridges two cases — locative of time relativized, accusative of time declared.

8.23.title: अनावृत्तिम् आवृत्तिम् च — the antithetical pair
8.23.body: अनावृत्तिम् ("non-return") and आवृत्तिम् ("return") are द्वितीया एकवचन objects of यान्ति (लट् प्रथम बहुवचन — "they go"). The यान्ति + accusative-of-state construction is the same as याति मद्भावम् in 8.5 — Sanskrit takes the destination-as-state as the direct object.

8.24.title: Five nominatives in apposition — the bright list
8.24.body: अग्निः ज्योतिः अहः शुक्लः षण्मासाः उत्तरायणम् — six items in प्रथमा (with उत्तरायणम् नपुंसक) listed without coordination, each a marker of the bright path. The asyndetic list is the standard Vedic and Upaniṣadic style for ritual cosmic equations; the Gītā here recapitulates the Chāndogya devayāna.

8.24.title: ब्रह्म ब्रह्मविदः — the cognate accusative
8.24.body: ब्रह्म (नपुंसक द्वितीया एकवचन — accusative of goal with गच्छन्ति) is reached by ब्रह्मविदः (प्रथमा बहुवचन — "knowers of Brahman," तत्पुरुष). The repetition of the same stem in both the goal and the goer is intentional — knowing-Brahman leads to Brahman, an etymological closure.

8.25.title: Parallel structure mirrors the dark path
8.25.body: धूमः रात्रिः कृष्णः षण्मासाः दक्षिणायनम् — five nominatives exactly parallel to 8.24, replacing fire with smoke, day with night, bright with dark, northern with southern. The structural parallelism makes the two paths visually identical in syntax but opposite in destination.

8.25.title: चान्द्रमसम् ज्योतिः ... निवर्तते — the returning yogin
8.25.body: चान्द्रमसम् ज्योतिः (द्वितीया एकवचन with प्राप्य — "having attained the lunar light") and निवर्तते (लट् आत्मनेपद of नि + √वृत् — "returns") together define the lower path as one whose terminus is the moon, after which return is mandatory — the precise opposite of 8.21's न निवर्तन्ते.

8.26.title: शुक्लकृष्णे गती — the dual of cosmic paths
8.26.body: शुक्लकृष्णे is द्विवचन of a द्वन्द्व/कर्मधारय compound; गती is स्त्रीलिंग द्विवचन प्रथमा. The dual itself is the grammatical marker that these are exactly two paths, neither more nor fewer. शाश्वते मते — both in द्विवचन agreeing with गती — predicates eternity and traditional acceptance.

8.26.title: एकया ... अन्यया — the instrumental antithesis
8.26.body: एकया ... अन्यया are तृतीया एकवचन स्त्रीलिंग — "by the one ... by the other," the instrumental of means. यात्यनावृत्तिम् (एकया) and आवर्तते पुनः (अन्यया) — accusative of state vs. simple return. The chiastic distribution: one accusative-of-goal, one simple verb with adverb पुनः.

8.27.title: Two relative-correlative threads, one negation
8.27.body: नैते सृती (नपुंसक/स्त्री द्विवचन accusative — "these two paths") is the object of जानन् (शतृ — "knowing"); न मुह्यति (लट् of √मुह् — "is not deluded") is the finite. कश्चन (indefinite — "any whatsoever") strengthens the negation across all yogins.

8.27.title: योगयुक्तो भव — the imperative of practical conclusion
8.27.body: तस्मात् ... सर्वेषु कालेषु (पञ्चमी + सप्तमी बहुवचन) frames the imperative भव (लोट् मध्यम एकवचन of √भू). The predicate योगयुक्तः is a बहुव्रीहि ("one yoked to yoga"). The chapter ends with the same practical command as 8.7 — anuṣaṅgika to fight, here simply to be yoked.

8.28.title: Four locatives of ritual merit
8.28.body: वेदेषु यज्ञेषु तपःसु दानेषु are all सप्तमी बहुवचन ("in Vedas, in sacrifices, in austerities, in gifts") — the standard catalogue of merit-producing acts. The locative here marks the domain in which the punya-phala has been prescribed (प्रदिष्टम् — क्त past passive participle of प्र + √दिश्).

8.28.title: अत्येति ... इदम् विदित्वा — transcending by knowing
8.28.body: अत्येति (लट् of अति + √इ — "goes beyond, transcends") governs तत् सर्वम् (नपुंसक द्वितीया — "all that"). The means is the absolutive विदित्वा ("having known") with इदम् as its object — the entire teaching of chapter 8. The यत् ... तत् correlative then yields परं स्थानम् आद्यम् — "the highest, primal abode" — as the final destination.
